one_line_takeaway: TOSSS reframes LLM security evaluation from hard-to-score code generation into a simple, CVE-mined A/B secure-vs-vulnerable selection task, and finds 14 top models score 0.48-0.88 hintless, that a security hint usually helps (but hurts Codestral), and that coding-specialized models are surprisingly weak at picking the secure option.
key_finding: Across 14 models, hintless C/C++ scores range from 0.480 (Mistral Large 2512, essentially random) to 0.878 (GLM-5); hintless Java ranges 0.488 to 0.846 (GPT-5.4 top). Most models show at least a weak preference for secure code even without being told security is the objective. Adding an explicit security hint helps on average (+0.021 C/C++, +0.029 Java) and helps weaker models most (Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite and Mistral Large 2512 each +0.068 on C/C++), while top models barely move (GPT-5.4/GLM-5/LLaMA 3 70B +0.002). A notable counter-result: coding-specialized models (Qwen3 Coder Next, Codestral 2508) rank in the bottom four, and Codestral 2508 gets WORSE with the security hint (-0.072 C/C++, -0.066 Java) -- suggesting code-generation optimization does not translate to secure-code discrimination. Within the Anthropic family, scores rise monotonically with generation (Claude 3.5 Sonnet 0.752 < Sonnet 4.6 0.808 < Opus 4.6 0.864 on C/C++).
core_contribution: The paper introduces TOSSS (Two-Option Secure Snippet Selection), a benchmark that measures whether LLMs can distinguish secure from vulnerable code. Its own novelty is a reformulation of the evaluation task: instead of asking a model to GENERATE code (the dominant paradigm in the 15 prior benchmarks the authors survey in Table 1), TOSSS presents two versions of the same function -- one vulnerable, one with the security fix applied -- and asks the model to pick one. The security score is simply the proportion of times the model picks the secure version. Test-case pairs are mined automatically from the CVE database via the pre-existing MegaVul pipeline (function code before/after a fixing commit), which the authors argue makes the benchmark extensible (new CVEs/languages ingested automatically), scalable, consistent (output constrained to 'A'/'B', no code-extraction parsing failures), and interpretable (a probabilistic score where ~0.5 = random guessing and <0.5 = a preference for vulnerable code). Note: the MegaVul dataset and pipeline are prior work (Ni et al. [21]) that TOSSS reuses; the paper's contribution is the selection-task benchmark framing and the evaluation of 14 models, not the mining pipeline itself.
threat_model: The motivating risk (stated in the paper, not invented) is that LLMs are now widely used as coding assistants and can introduce security vulnerabilities into software, weakening existing security efforts. The paper cites prior findings (Pearce et al.) that 40% of 1,689 Copilot-generated programs contained vulnerabilities -- this is cited context, not the paper's own result. TOSSS operationalizes 'is this model good at software security?' as a measurable capability. The authors explicitly frame a score significantly below 0.5 as 'possibly malicious behavior' (a systematic preference for vulnerable code). The benchmark itself is a measurement instrument, not an attack or defense.
limitations: Acknowledged/evident limitations: (1) Coverage is limited to C/C++ and Java because that is what the reused MegaVul dataset contains; extension to other languages is left as future work. (2) The core task (selecting between two given implementations) is not how developers actually use LLMs (they generate/complete); the authors defend selection only as a 'prerequisite' minimal capability for secure generation, analogous to membership inference as a minimal privacy-leakage signal. (3) Benchmark contamination / data-reuse risk: CVE-derived data may overlap with model training corpora, and the authors themselves note fine-tuning on such data would create evaluation concerns. (4) Only 500 functions per language per model were evaluated to limit cost, so scores are estimates over a subsample of the ~17k available. (5) The 'secure' (post-fix) snippet is assumed correct ground truth, but a post-fix function could still contain other latent vulnerabilities, and pairs may differ in non-security ways the model keys on. (6) The <0.5 = 'possibly malicious' framing is an interpretive claim, not independently validated.

datasets_used: MegaVul dataset (Ni et al. [21]) -- ~17,000 vulnerable functions in C, C++, and Java, with function code before/after each CVE fix; reused as-is
Evaluation subsample: 500 C/C++ functions and 500 Java functions per model (same entries/order across all models)
quantitative_results: Hintless C/C++ scores range 0.480 (Mistral Large 2512) to 0.878 (GLM-5)
Hintless Java scores range 0.488 (Mistral Large 2512) to 0.846 (GPT-5.4)
Top-4 hintless C/C++: GLM-5 0.878, GPT-5.4 0.866, Claude Opus 4.6 0.864, Kimi K2.5 0.858
Mean hint improvement: +0.021 (C/C++), +0.029 (Java); largest gains Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite +0.068 and Mistral Large 2512 +0.068 on C/C++
Codestral 2508 degrades with the security hint: -0.072 (C/C++), -0.066 (Java)
Anthropic family monotonic on hintless C/C++: Claude 3.5 Sonnet 0.752, Sonnet 4.6 0.808, Opus 4.6 0.864
Mistral Large 2512 hintless C/C++ 0.480 is statistically comparable to the random-guess baseline of 0.5
baselines_compared: Random guessing (score = 0.5) as the reference for the interpretability of the metric

14 evaluated LLMs compared against each other: GLM-5, GPT-5.4, Claude Opus 4.6, Kimi K2.5, MiniMax M2.5, Gemini 3 Flash, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, LLaMA 3 70B, Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite, Codestral 2508, DeepSeek V3.2, Qwen3 Coder Next, Mistral Large 2512

Methodological comparison (Table 1) against 15 prior secure-coding benchmarks (e.g., CyberSecEval, SALLM, LLMSecCode, CodeLMSec, SecRepoBench, A.S.E, SecureAgentBench, SafeGenBench, CWEval, LLM-CSEC, RealSec-Bench), contrasting their generation/static-analyzer approach with TOSSS's selection/ground-truth approach
